content:
7 min read
Just now

--

A website with no names, no followers, no feed, no scoreboard. Just stories from those who stood where you’re standing.
There’s a specific kind of 3 a.m. search that most of us have done at least once.
You’ve closed the group chat. You’ve decided you’re not calling anyone, not tonight. So you open a browser and you type something into it that you would never say out loud, and you hope — half-embarrassed, fully desperate — that somewhere in the machine there’s a person who has already been here.
What you get back is: a listicle. Seven ways to cope. A therapy app ad. A forum thread from 2013 where the top comment is “have you tried journaling.” A subreddit where the most upvoted reply is the funniest one(hate this when things are serious), not the truest one. An influencer with a ring light explaining her healing era.
None of it is what you were looking for. What you were looking for is very small and very specific: evidence that a person survived this exact thing, told in their own voice, without trying to sell you anything.
That’s the whole idea behind what I’m building.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Phaanoos landing pagePhaanoos فانوس
is the lantern. Not a decorative one — the communal kind, the one that belongs to the neighborhood rather than to any one house. Kids carry them door to door. Nobody signs them. The point isn’t whose lantern it is. The point is that the street is less dark than it was.
That’s the metaphor, and I’ve been ruthless about letting it govern the product. If a feature doesn’t fit the lantern, it doesn’t ship.
The site does two things:
Light a Lantern. You write, anonymously, about something hard you have lived through. Not a LinkedIn “grateful for the lessons” post. Just the true account of what it was like and what actually helped you carry it.
Pick a Lantern. You tell the site, roughly- if you want, where you are right now. It hands you stories from those who has been somewhere similar. You read it, feel it. That’s it. There’s nothing to do afterward.
Tagline, which I’ve rewritten about forty times and keep coming back to:
From those who made it — to those still trying.
The features I deliberately did not build
This is the part people find strange, so let me just list it.

• No comments. Nobody replies to your worst night with “sending hugs” or, worse, advice.

• No usernames, no profiles, no avatars. Not “anonymous handles.” Actually anonymous.

• No follower counts, no trending, no “most read this week.”

• No ads. No tracking. No engagement optimization.
Every single one of those is a thing I could add tomorrow, and every single one of them would make the site more successful by the metrics the internet uses and worse at the only job it has.
Here’s my reasoning, and you can tell me if it’s wrong.
Anonymity isn’t a privacy feature here. It’s the precondition for the good stuff. The stories that would help you most at 3 a.m. are exactly the stories nobody will publish under their own name. The relapse. The resentment toward the person you were caring for. The relief when they died. The two years you spent unemployed and lied about. The moment you understood your marriage was over. Attach a name to those and the story quietly changes shape — it becomes performance, becomes reputation management, becomes a version the author can live with being seen holding. I would rather have the true version from someone I’ll never identify.
Also, I don’t want to gather that type of data that makes people greedy to look for it later. I don’t gather names, emails, specific locations and etc.
The two buttons, and why I think they survive my own rules
There are two options under a story:
The first one says This lit my way. You only press if the story kinda warmed your heart and lit your night.
The second button says Flag, and it goes to me. Not to a queue, not to a model, not to a majority vote — to a person who reads every one. For now, I’d rather the site be small enough that a human still reads it.
I’ll grant the honest objection: putting any button under a story turns the reader into an evaluator for half a second, and that’s not nothing. I decided it was worth it, because the writers gave something real away for free and deserve to know it landed. If it turns out I was wrong, the button comes off.
The question I ask writers
This one took me a long time to get right, and it’s the thing I’d most want another builder to steal.
The obvious prompt is: “How did you overcome this?”
I threw it out. It smuggles in a lie. It assumes there was an overcoming — a before and an after, a clean edge. For most of the hard things people actually go through, that edge doesn’t exist. You don’t get over losing your mother. You don’t graduate from anxiety. Ask someone to narrate a resolution they haven’t had, and they will invent one, politely, to be useful. Now you’ve got a library of tidy arcs, and a reader in the middle of the mess feels further behind than when they arrived.
So the prompt is:
What helped you through?
Different question entirely. It doesn’t require the weight to be gone. It just asks what made it possible to keep walking, which is a thing people can answer honestly on a Tuesday, mid-mess, without having to lie about the ending.
The answers I’ve collected so far are almost never advice. They’re specific and small. Someone’s brother who called every single day for a year and never once asked how they were doing, just talked about football. The trick of keeping the same alarm even when there was nothing to get up for. The permission a stranger gave them to stop forgiving somebody.
You cannot get that from a listicle. You can only get it from someone who was there.
What’s actually on the shelf
Ten rooms so far, roughly, plus the tags underneath them:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Grief & loss. Heartbreak & divorce. Depression & anxiety. Addiction & recovery. Job loss & money. Illness & health. Loneliness & isolation. Family conflict. Immigration & displacement. Burnout & pressure.
And under those, the phrases that do the real matching, because “grief” is too big to be useful and “anniversary waves” or “guilt & what-ifs” is not: can’t get out of bed. high-functioning but empty. still love them. two worlds, belonging to neither. relapse. starting from zero.
That’s the part I care most about getting right. Not how many stories are in the library — how accurately the right one finds the right person. A hundred well-matched lanterns beat ten thousand badly-matched ones, and I’ll take a smaller, slower, better-sorted collection every time.
What it isn’t
Being clear about this matters more than being appealing about it.
Phaanoos is not therapy. It is not crisis support. It is not advice, and it is not a place to make friends. If you’re in danger tonight, it is the wrong website, and it will tell you so and point you somewhere better.
What it is, at most, is the thing a good book can do — the moment on page 60 where a stranger describes your exact interior weather and you realize you are not uniquely broken, just a person, in a category of people, and some of them made it through.
That’s a modest claim. I’d rather make a modest claim honestly than a big one loosely.
If you’ve made it through something
I’m collecting the first stories now, deliberately slowly, mostly by asking people directly. Not because I want an exclusive launch — because I want to read every single one myself before anyone else does, and I want the matching to be good before the shelf is full.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

phaanoos.com/the-wallIf you’re carrying something you got through — or something you’re still getting through, and you’ve figured out anything at all about how to carry it — that’s a lantern. It costs you a few hundred words and your name stays yours.
Someone is going to be awake at 3 a.m. typing the thing they can’t say out loud. Right now the internet hands them a listicle.
I’d like it to hand them you.
phaanoos.com — From those who made it, to those still trying.
